message:
docs: mesh-first README reframing, production deployment runbook, and beads tracker sync

README reframing from host-first to mesh-first truthful transition:
- Rewrite the "what is this" framing to teach the platform from the mesh
  outward: mesh-backed answers are the highest-confidence runtime truth,
  host-backed answers are the current node-local control-plane view, and
  offline artifacts are preparation/debugging surfaces
- Reorder the feature table to lead with Mesh-Native Architecture and
  Symbol-First Protocol, adding Truthful Runtime Resolution as a feature
  row and repositioning host-first as "Host-First Control Plane (Current)"
- Add diagram context note explaining the steady-state intent vs the
  current transition control plane
- Rewrite Quick Example to start with the runtime truth question
  (mesh explain-availability, status, invoke) before offline preparation
- Update FWC Truth Model section to explain the knowledge-state taxonomy
  (mesh-backed, host-backed, node-local, offline, degraded, fallback-derived)
  as the primary resolution model rather than a host-only framing
- Add reference to the transition-specific nature of the playbook
- Add complete Production Mesh Deployment Runbook section with:
  - Proof anchors table linking acceptance contracts, evidence index, and
    integration tests
  - Current honest topology (single-active-host, warm-standby mesh)
  - Minimum bring-up sequence with rch remote compilation
  - Provisioning and secret flow with config export/import safety rules
  - Rollout and rollback loop with explicit stabilization check sequence
  - Failover assumptions and what NOT to claim without new proof artifacts
- Update Current Implementation Topology to describe host-first as the
  current supervision boundary rather than the permanent model
- Update Known Limitations to say "single-active-host" instead of
  "no production mesh deployment"

FWC Host-First Truthfulness Playbook:
- Add Production Deployment Runbook section (parallel to README) with:
  - Proof anchors table with evidence surfaces and their purposes
  - Current honest topology table (active host, standby peer, mesh peers)
  - Explicit assumptions about local admin state and mutation flow
  - Provisioning sequence with remote compilation and state paths
  - Secret and config flow with export/import/doctor sequence
  - Bring-up verification loop with interpretation guidance
  - Rollout and rollback sequence with truth-contract-literal reading
  - Stabilization check loop
  - Failover and promotion checklist with explicit non-claims
  - Evidence and diagnosis bundle order plus platform proof rerun commands

Beads tracker: sync issue metadata to current state

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-3z2","title":"fcp.spotify: Library Management","description":"# fcp.spotify: Library Management (FCP2)

## Goal
Enable safe access to the user’s library:
- list saved tracks/albums/shows
- save/unsave items

Library writes modify user state and must be auditable.

## Scope
- Get saved tracks/albums/shows/episodes (pagination).
- Save/unsave items.
- Check whether items are saved.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `spotify.library.read`
- `spotify.library.write` (dangerous-ish)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- For writes:
  - receipts + audit events include item ids and action
  - never log track names or personal context

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `spotify.library.tracks.list`
  - `spotify.library.tracks.save`
  - `spotify.library.tracks.remove`
  - `spotify.library.albums.list`
  - `spotify.library.albums.save/remove`
  - `spotify.library.shows.list/save/remove`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - pagination
  - save/unsave idempotency
  - audit/receipt for writes

## Acceptance criteria
- Library operations are reliable, safe, and auditable.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b","title":"Testing Completeness Program: non-mock acceptance coverage and high-signal E2E evidence","description":"Background:
- The repo already has meaningful testing assets: `crates/fcp-e2e`, `crates/fcp-host/tests/host_connector_integration.rs`, `fcp-testkit`, connector `tests/integration.rs`, and structured JSONL reporting.
- That is still not the same thing as full pure-unit coverage without mocks/fake peers or complete end-to-end acceptance coverage with operator-grade logs.
- The current 2026-03-23 inventory says the workspace has 149 connector directories and 28 core crates; 139/149 connectors show unit-test signal, 100/149 have crate-local `tests/`, 84/149 have `fcp-e2e` coverage, 0/149 expose connector-level `no_mock_integration.rs`, only 8 connectors document verification bundles, and logging/replay evidence exists for 92/149 connectors.
- The user question behind this program is therefore settled: no, the repo does not yet have full unit coverage without fake-backed confidence, and no, it does not yet have complete non-mock E2E scripts with great detailed logging across the workspace.

Program goal:
- Make pure-unit coverage explicit and truthful, with fake-backed suites demoted to `deterministic_contract` rather than silently counting as final unit evidence.
- Give every core crate and connector family an archetype-appropriate acceptance path: `local_non_mock`, `host_e2e`, `live`, or a documented combination.
- Make every E2E-capable suite emit durable, redacted, replayable artifacts that let future agents debug failures without rediscovering the whole test setup.

Definition of done for the overall program:
- The coverage matrix and acceptance taxonomy stay current and are enforced.
- Shared harnesses exist for HTTP, streaming/webhook/session, and DB/storage/queue/browser/local-process boundaries.
- Connector-family sweeps close both pure-unit floor gaps and non-mock acceptance gaps.
- Nightly/live suites have an explicit operating model for secrets, cost, cleanup, and artifact retention.
- CI and maintainer playbooks make the whole testing story executable rather than aspirational.","notes":"Clarification: repo policy still forbids real external-service calls in unit tests. This program treats "no mocks/fake stuff" as a requirement for acceptance layers (`local_non_mock`, `host_e2e`, `live`) while `pure_unit` stays focused on local logic and truthful suite classification.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-23T08:04:33.507188Z","created_by":"LilacLake","updated_at":"2026-03-25T00:34:42.461132Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T00:34:42.460968Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","quality","testing"]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b.1","title":"Audit current workspace test surface and publish a connector/core coverage gap matrix","description":"Background:
- Before changing test infrastructure, we need one authoritative inventory of what exists today.
- The current workspace mixes inline unit tests, `tests/integration.rs`, crate-level `no_mock_integration.rs`, `fcp-e2e` compliance suites, host subprocess tests, and many `wiremock`/`MockApiServer` suites.

Deliverables:
- Build a connector-by-connector and core-crate-by-core-crate matrix that records: unit tests present, integration tests present, `wiremock`/fake reliance, `no_mock_integration` presence, host-backed subprocess coverage, `fcp-e2e` coverage, logging/report schema validation, live/provider prerequisites, and known gaps.
- Explicitly classify which current tests are deterministic contract tests versus true non-mock acceptance tests.
- Group results by connector archetype so later waves can execute systematically instead of ad hoc.

Acceptance criteria:
- The matrix covers every current connector directory and every core crate that participates in runtime, policy, host, protocol, or observability behavior.
- Missing suites are prioritized by risk and user impact, not just by alphabetical order.
- The output is self-contained enough that future agents can plan from the matrix without repeating the archaeology pass.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"ChartreuseDesert","created_at":"2026-03-23T08:04:33.794376Z","created_by":"LilacLake","updated_at":"2026-03-23T08:31:23.144998Z","closed_at":"2026-03-23T08:31:23.144658Z","close_reason":"Published live test coverage and acceptance gap matrix in docs/testing/coverage-inventory.md","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["coverage","e2e","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b.1","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-23T08:04:33.794376Z","created_by":"LilacLake","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b.10","title":"Developer-platform and automation connectors: non-mock acceptance wave","description":"Background:
- Developer-platform and automation connectors often look easy because they are HTTP APIs, but they also have rollout, artifact, webhook, and long-running job semantics that simple mocks miss.

Scope:
- GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, CircleCI, Package Registry, DockerHub, Make, n8n, Browser, MCP Bridge, Webhook Receiver, Vercel, Netlify, and related developer-platform or automation connectors.

Deliverables:
- Add non-mock acceptance coverage for auth, polling/job-state transitions, webhook or callback flows, artifact handling, pagination, and mutation safety semantics.
- Validate host-backed operation flows end to end, including logging and receipt/approval behavior where relevant.
- Close missing compliance E2E gaps in this family and demote mock-only suites to supplemental status.

Acceptance criteria:
- Developer-platform connectors are covered by acceptance suites that exercise real workflows, not just static HTTP payload fixtures.
- Failures produce enough detail to debug CI/automation regressions without manual repro.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-23T08:04:36.815187Z","created_by":"LilacLake","updated_at":"2026-03-24T06:03:22Z","closed_at":"2026-03-24T06:03:22Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["developer-platform","e2e","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b.10","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-23T08:04:36.815187Z","created_by":"LilacLake","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b.10","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-23T08:04:43.323043Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b.10","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-23T08:04:43.562472Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b.10","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-23T08:04:43.807886Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b.10","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-23T08:04:44.073569Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b.10","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-49z0b.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-23T08:04:44.315094Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-pl7pj","title":"[FCP3/P6.4] Rewrite docs, examples, and transcripts so mesh backed operation is taught as normal","description":"Background: documentation and transcripts shape how future contributors think about the architecture. If they keep teaching host first operation as the normal model, the implementation will drift back toward that shape even after code changes land.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-ptr","title":"fcp.anthropic - Anthropic AI Provider Connector","description":"# FCP Anthropic Connector - Anthropic AI Provider (LEGACY / SUPERSEDED)

> NOTE (2026-01-15): This bead predates the current **FCP2 connector model** and is kept only for historical context of an earlier prototype.
> 
> The active, FCP2-compliant Anthropic plan is: `flywheel_connectors-lszk.4` (and its child beads). Use that as the source of truth.

## Overview
A comprehensive connector for Anthropic's Claude API, enabling agents to leverage Claude models for complex reasoning, tool use, vision capabilities, and batch processing with built-in usage tracking and budget enforcement.

## Priority: P1 (Critical Infrastructure)

## Core Capabilities

### 1. Messages API
- Send messages to Claude models
- Streaming responses for real-time output
- Multi-turn conversations
- System prompts
- Model selection (claude-opus-4-5-20251101, claude-sonnet-4-20250514, claude-haiku, etc.)
- Temperature and sampling controls
- Max tokens configuration
- Stop sequences

### 2. Tool Use Support
- Define tools with JSON schema
- Handle tool_use responses
- Execute tools and return results
- Multi-tool conversations
- Parallel tool execution
- Tool choice forcing (auto, any, specific)

### 3. Vision Capabilities
- Images in prompts (base64 or URL)
- Multiple images per message
- Image analysis and description
- Document/screenshot understanding
- Chart and diagram interpretation

### 4. Prompt Caching
- Cache system prompts
- Cache large context documents
- Cache tool definitions
- Automatic cache management
- Cache hit/miss tracking
- Usage/cost savings can be computed from tracked usage deltas

### 5. Batch API
- Submit batch requests
- Track batch status
- Retrieve batch results
- Cancel batches
- Batch pricing can change; treat billing as provider- or policy-owned (do not hardcode discounts)

### 6. Usage Tracking & Budget Enforcement
- Per-request usage calculation (tokens, images, audio seconds, etc.)
- Running total tracking
- Budget limits per agent/session/day
- Alerts at thresholds
- Automatic cutoff at budget limit
- Usage attribution and reporting

## FCP Integration

### Manifest Structure
```toml
[connector]
name = "fcp.anthropic"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Anthropic Claude API integration with usage tracking"

[capabilities]
messages = { risk = "medium", description = "Send messages to Claude" }
streaming = { risk = "medium", description = "Stream responses" }
tool_use = { risk = "medium", description = "Use tools in conversations" }
vision = { risk = "medium", description = "Process images" }
batch = { risk = "low", description = "Submit batch requests" }

[trust_zones]
default = "z:ai:inference"

[resources]
rate_limit_rpm = 1000
rate_limit_tpm = 100000
default_timeout_ms = 120000

[billing]
track_costs = true
budget_enforcement = true
```

### Tool Definitions
```yaml
tools:
  - name: anthropic_message
    description: Send a message to Claude
    parameters:
      model:
        type: string
        enum: [claude-opus-4-5-20251101, claude-sonnet-4-20250514, claude-3-5-haiku-20241022]
        required: true
      messages:
        type: array
        items:
          type: object
          properties:
            role: { type: string, enum: [user, assistant] }
            content: { type: string }
        required: true
      system: { type: string }
      max_tokens: { type: integer, default: 4096 }
      temperature: { type: number, default: 1.0, min: 0, max: 1 }
      stream: { type: boolean, default: false }

  - name: anthropic_message_with_tools
    description: Send a message with tool definitions
    parameters:
      model: { type: string, required: true }
      messages: { type: array, required: true }
      tools:
        type: array
        items:
          type: object
          properties:
            name: { type: string }
            description: { type: string }
            input_schema: { type: object }
      tool_choice:
        type: object
        properties:
          type: { type: string, enum: [auto, any, tool] }
          name: { type: string }

  - name: anthropic_message_with_vision
    description: Send a message with images
    parameters:
      model: { type: string, required: true }
      messages:
        type: array
        items:
          type: object
          properties:
            role: { type: string }
            content:
              type: array
              items:
                oneOf:
                  - type: object
                    properties:
                      type: { const: text }
                      text: { type: string }
                  - type: object
                    properties:
                      type: { const: image }
                      source:
                        type: object
                        properties:
                          type: { enum: [base64, url] }
                          media_type: { type: string }
                          data: { type: string }

  - name: anthropic_batch_create
    description: Create a batch request
    parameters:
      requests:
        type: array
        items:
          type: object
          properties:
            custom_id: { type: string }
            params: { type: object }
        required: true

  - name: anthropic_batch_status
    description: Get batch status
    parameters:
      batch_id: { type: string, required: true }

  - name: anthropic_batch_results
    description: Get batch results
    parameters:
      batch_id: { type: string, required: true }

  - name: anthropic_get_usage
    description: Get current usage metrics (currency cost is policy/ops-owned)
    parameters:
      period: { type: string, enum: [session, day, month] }
```

## Usage / Cost Tracking (NO hard-coded pricing)

### Why we do NOT embed pricing tables
Provider pricing changes frequently. Hard-coding a “pricing model” table in a connector/spec becomes incorrect quickly.

### What we track instead (stable)
- provider-reported usage fields (when present)
- token counts (input/output/cache)
- image counts/size classes
- audio seconds

### Budget enforcement
Budgets can be enforced in two stable ways:
1. **Usage budgets** (preferred): token/image/second limits per request/session/day.
2. **Currency budgets** (optional): computed by an external, versioned “pricing catalog” object owned by policy/ops (not embedded in the connector).

### Attribution
- Track usage per agent
- Track usage per task/session
- Track usage per operation/tool
- Generate reports

## Prompt Caching Strategy
- System prompts > 1024 tokens: Always cache
- Tool definitions: Cache when > 5 tools
- Large documents: Cache with TTL
- Cache warming on connector init

## Error Handling
- Rate limit (429): Exponential backoff
- Overloaded (529): Queue and retry
- Invalid request (400): Parse and report
- Auth failure (401): Refresh credentials
- Context length exceeded: Truncation strategy

## Dependencies
- anthropic-rs or custom HTTP client
- reqwest (HTTP)
- tokio (async)
- serde (serialization)
- async-stream (streaming)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Send basic message and receive response
- [ ] Stream response tokens in real-time
- [ ] Use tools in conversation
- [ ] Process images in prompts
- [ ] Submit and retrieve batch jobs
- [ ] Track usage accurately per request
- [ ] Enforce budget limits
- [ ] Handle rate limits gracefully
- [ ] Utilize prompt caching

+## Production Mesh Deployment Runbook
+
+This repository now has a truthful deployment/runbook surface for the current
+platform shape. Read it with two constraints in mind:
+
+1. the steady-state architectural target is still mesh-native
+2. the proven operator path today is still `fwc -> fcp-host -> connector subprocesses`
+
+The guide is therefore about running the current system honestly, not
+pretending the future cutover is already complete.
+
+### Proof Anchors
+
+Treat these as the evidence bundle for deployment claims:
+
+- [docs/FWC_Host_First_Truthfulness_Playbook.md](docs/FWC_Host_First_Truthfulness_Playbook.md) for the operator truth model, replay contract, and deployment/failover checklist
+- [docs/FCP3_Acceptance_Contracts.md](docs/FCP3_Acceptance_Contracts.md) for the phase-5/phase-6 proof obligations behind mesh-backed and host-backed claims
+- [docs/testing/core_platform_evidence_index.md](docs/testing/core_platform_evidence_index.md) for the rerun commands that verify the platform crates backing the operator story
+
+### Current Honest Topology
+
+| Role | Current responsibility | What it proves |
+|------|------------------------|----------------|
+| **Active host node** | Runs `fcp-host`, supervises connector subprocesses, owns the live connector inventory file and lifecycle state snapshot | Authoritative live `fwc` answers for status, doctor, rollout, config, and invoke |
+| **Standby host-capable peer** | Has the same connector binaries, manifests, policy objects, and deployment artifacts staged, but is not treated as authoritative until promoted | Promotion readiness, not current live truth |
+| **Mesh/object peers** | Hold placement targets, policy/evidence objects, and durability context used by `mesh explain-availability` | Whether a live answer can be elevated from merely host-backed to mesh-backed |
+
+Today this should be operated as a **single-active-host** system with
+deliberate promotion of a standby peer. Connector lifecycle/admin state is
+still persisted locally by `fcp-host`, so active/active control-plane teaching
+would be dishonest.
+
+### Minimum Bring-Up
+
+Build the operator binaries with remote compilation:
+
+```bash
+rch exec -- cargo build -p fcp-host -p fwc --release
+```
+
+Start `fcp-host` with explicit operator-state paths:
+
+```bash
+export FCP_HOST_BIND=0.0.0.0:8787
+export FCP_HOST_CONNECTORS_FILE=/srv/fcp/connectors.json
+export FCP_HOST_LIFECYCLE_STATE_FILE=/srv/fcp/lifecycle-state.json
+
+./target/release/fcp-host
+```
+
+Then verify the deployment from an operator shell:
+
+```bash
+fwc --host http://127.0.0.1:8787 list
+fwc --host http://127.0.0.1:8787 mesh explain-availability github
+fwc --host http://127.0.0.1:8787 status github
+fwc --host http://127.0.0.1:8787 doctor --zone z:work --all
+fwc config doctor github --host http://127.0.0.1:8787
+```
+
+Healthy interpretation:
+
+- `list`, `status`, `doctor`, `config doctor`, and rollout/config mutations are
+  authoritative only when they come from the live host
+- `mesh explain-availability` is the command that can legitimately elevate a
+  connector from host-backed/node-local truth to mesh-backed truth
+- if `mesh explain-availability` does not report mesh-backed readiness, do not
+  describe the deployment as fully mesh-backed yet
+
+### Provisioning And Secret Flow
+
+- Treat `FCP_HOST_CONNECTORS_FILE` as the live connector inventory source that
+  `fcp-host` mutates.
+- Treat `FCP_HOST_LIFECYCLE_STATE_FILE` as the local admin-state snapshot that
+  must move with the active host during a controlled failover.
+- Stage the same connector binaries and manifests on the standby peer before
+  claiming failover readiness.
+- Use `fwc config export <connector> --host ... --file baseline.json` before
+  any risky config change.
+- Use `fwc config import <connector> --host ... --file candidate.json` for live
+  config mutation, then immediately run `fwc config doctor`.
+- If `fwc config export` reports a sanitized non-replayable snapshot, move the
+  affected secrets into credential references or rebuild a complete config
+  document explicitly; do not assume the sanitized export is a rollback file.
+
+### Rollout And Rollback Loop
+
+Use the rollout surface as the operator control loop:
+
+```bash
+fwc rollout set github --canary 10 --host http://127.0.0.1:8787
+fwc rollout status github --host http://127.0.0.1:8787
+fwc status github --host http://127.0.0.1:8787
+fwc doctor --zone z:work --all --host http://127.0.0.1:8787
+fwc rollout rollback github --to 1.2.2 --host http://127.0.0.1:8787
+```
+
+Operational rules:
+
+- `rollout set` and `rollout rollback` are truthful for the current node's live
+  mutation, but they do not by themselves prove later runtime stabilization
+- after every rollout or rollback, re-check `rollout status`, `status`,
+  `doctor`, and `mesh explain-availability`
+- if the active node degrades, promote the staged standby peer deliberately
+  rather than assuming automatic lease handoff or automatic state convergence
+
+### Failover Assumptions
+
+The current deployment guide is intentionally narrower than the long-term mesh
+vision:
+
+- multi-node durable-object placement is part of the evidence story
+- connector admin/lifecycle state is still node-local
+- automatic lease handoff and multi-node connector-state replication remain
+  future proof obligations, not current production promises
+
+That means the current honest production story is **warm-standby mesh-backed
+operation with supervised promotion**, not autonomous active/active mesh
+control-plane failover.

+## Production Deployment Runbook
+
+This section is the operator-facing deployment guide for the current truthful
+platform shape. It is intentionally narrower than the long-term mesh-native
+vision: it teaches how to run the system that exists now, without fabricating
+automatic multi-node properties that the codebase does not yet prove.
+
+### Proof anchors
+
+Use these artifacts together when you need to justify a deployment claim:
+
+| Evidence surface | Why it matters |
+|---|---|
+| `docs/FCP3_Acceptance_Contracts.md` | Names the phase-5 mesh and phase-6 CLI truth proof obligations |
+| `docs/testing/core_platform_evidence_index.md` | Lists the rerun commands for the platform crates behind the operator story |
+| `crates/fwc/tests/cual_integration.rs` | Freezes operator-truth fixture classes, including `mesh_backed`, `node_local`, `offline`, and `degraded` |
+| `crates/fcp-host/tests/host_connector_integration.rs` | Covers rollout, rollback, config mutation, and host-backed lifecycle/admin flows |
+
+If the guide below and those proof surfaces diverge, trust the proof surfaces
+and update the guide.
+
+### Current honest topology
+
+Treat production today as a **single-active-host, warm-standby mesh**.
+
+| Role | Must exist | Current truthful responsibility |
+|---|---|---|
+| Active host node | Yes | Runs `fcp-host`, supervises connectors, owns the authoritative live admin state |
+| Standby host-capable peer | Strongly recommended | Holds the same binaries, manifests, and deployment artifacts so promotion is deliberate and fast |
+| Mesh/object peers | Yes for mesh-backed claims | Supply placement/durability context that can elevate a live answer from host-backed to mesh-backed |
+
+Current assumptions that must stay explicit:
+
+- all live operator mutations still flow through the active `fcp-host`
+- `FCP_HOST_CONNECTORS_FILE` is the live connector inventory mutation surface
+- `FCP_HOST_LIFECYCLE_STATE_FILE` is the local admin-state snapshot that must
+  move with the promoted host
+- connector admin/lifecycle state is still node-local, so active/active
+  control-plane claims would be dishonest
+
+### Provisioning sequence
+
+1. Build release binaries with remote compilation.
+   ```bash
+   rch exec -- cargo build -p fcp-host -p fwc --release
+   ```
+2. Stage the same connector binaries and manifests on the active node and at
+   least one standby peer.
+3. Provision explicit host-state paths on the active node.
+   ```bash
+   export FCP_HOST_BIND=0.0.0.0:8787
+   export FCP_HOST_CONNECTORS_FILE=/srv/fcp/connectors.json
+   export FCP_HOST_LIFECYCLE_STATE_FILE=/srv/fcp/lifecycle-state.json
+   ./target/release/fcp-host
+   ```
+4. Keep the connector inventory file and lifecycle state file under your normal
+   deployment backup/replication discipline. They are the current operator truth
+   surfaces for live inventory and lifecycle history.
+
+### Secret and config flow
+
+Configuration changes should always preserve a replayable rollback path.
+
+1. Export the live baseline before changing anything.
+   ```bash
+   fwc config export github --host http://127.0.0.1:8787 --file baseline.json
+   ```
+2. Validate the current live snapshot.
+   ```bash
+   fwc config doctor github --host http://127.0.0.1:8787
+   ```
+3. Import the candidate config document.
+   ```bash
+   fwc config import github --host http://127.0.0.1:8787 --file candidate.json
+   ```
+4. Re-run `fwc config doctor`.
+
+Important limitation:
+
+- if `fwc config export` reports a sanitized non-replayable snapshot, the
+  exported file is inspection evidence only, not a rollback artifact
+- when that happens, either move secrets into credential references or prepare
+  a complete replacement config document explicitly before you mutate live state
+
+### Bring-up verification loop
+
+Run this minimum command set after provisioning and after every promotion:
+
+```bash
+fwc --host http://127.0.0.1:8787 list
+fwc --host http://127.0.0.1:8787 mesh explain-availability github
+fwc --host http://127.0.0.1:8787 status github
+fwc --host http://127.0.0.1:8787 doctor --zone z:work --all
+fwc config doctor github --host http://127.0.0.1:8787
+```
+
+How to interpret the answers:
+
+- `list`, `status`, `doctor`, `config doctor`, and rollout/config mutation
+  answers are authoritative only when they come from the live host
+- `mesh explain-availability` is the surface that can legitimately upgrade the
+  story from merely host-backed/node-local truth to mesh-backed truth
+- if the best honest answer is still `node_local`, `host_backed`, `degraded`,
+  or `offline`, do not describe the deployment as fully mesh-backed yet
+
+### Rollout and rollback sequence
+
+Use rollout commands as the live operator mutation path:
+
+```bash
+fwc rollout set github --canary 10 --host http://127.0.0.1:8787
+fwc rollout status github --host http://127.0.0.1:8787
+fwc status github --host http://127.0.0.1:8787
+fwc doctor --zone z:work --all --host http://127.0.0.1:8787
+fwc rollout rollback github --to 1.2.2 --host http://127.0.0.1:8787
+```
+
+Read the truth contract literally:
+
+- `rollout set` proves that the active node accepted the canary mutation
+- `rollout status` proves the current node-local rollout state snapshot
+- `rollout rollback` proves that the rollback mutation happened on the active node
+- none of those answers by themselves prove later runtime stabilization
+
+The stabilization check is always the same follow-up loop:
+
+1. `fwc rollout status ...`
+2. `fwc status ...`
+3. `fwc doctor --zone ... --all ...`
+4. `fwc mesh explain-availability ...`
+
+### Failover and promotion assumptions
+
+The current failover story is **supervised promotion**, not automatic mesh
+control-plane relocation.
+
+Use this checklist:
+
+1. Confirm the current active host is degraded or unavailable via `status`,
+   `doctor`, and `mesh explain-availability`.
+2. Promote the staged standby peer with the same connector binaries, manifests,
+   connector inventory file, and lifecycle state snapshot.
+3. Re-run the bring-up verification loop against the promoted host.
+4. Only after the promoted host returns truthful live answers should you route
+   normal operator traffic to it.
+
+Do not claim any of the following until new proof artifacts land:
+
+- automatic lease handoff between nodes
+- automatic multi-node connector-state convergence
+- active/active host mutation safety
+- post-promotion equivalence without re-running the verification loop
+
+### Evidence and diagnosis bundle
+
+When a deployment check or promotion drill fails, use the replayable evidence
+contract rather than ad hoc shell guessing.
+
+Start with the same bundle order described elsewhere in this playbook:
+
+1. `summary.json`
+2. `trace.jsonl`
+3. `environment.json`
+4. `replay.sh`
+
+Then re-run the platform proof surfaces that back this runbook:
+
+```bash
+rch exec -- cargo test -p fwc --test cual_integration
+rch exec -- cargo test -p fcp-host --test host_connector_integration
+rch exec -- cargo test -p fcp-e2e
+```
+
+Those commands do not prove a specific deployment is healthy, but they are the
+fastest way to separate a local deployment/configuration problem from a broken
+platform contract.
+
